subject: Science
big_idea: The biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere are interconnected, as matter cycles and energy flows through them
learning_goals: I can describe the four main Earth spheres.
I can identify when more than one sphere is involved in an event or change.
I can explain how a change in one sphere can affect another.

content.subject: Science
content.subtitle: How are Earth's spheres connected, and why does that matter when something changes?

scenario_lines.text: Heavy rain hits a steep hillside.
Soil washes into a river. The water gets muddy. Fish habitat changes.
What parts of Earth are involved here?

prompts: What parts of Earth seem connected?

rows.head: A system
rows.body: A system is made of parts that affect each other.

rows.head: A sphere
rows.body: Scientists use spheres as a model to organize connected parts of Earth.

rows.head: Big idea
rows.body: The spheres are not separate worlds. They help us explain how changes move through Earth systems.

columns.title: Atmosphere + hydrosphere

items.rest: rivers, lakes, rain, snow, ice, groundwater

items: Water can move through the air, on land, and through living systems.

columns.title: Geosphere + biosphere

items.rest: living things such as plants, animals, and people

items: One real event often involves more than one sphere at the same time.

content.header: Which sphere does each example fit best?

items.rest: hits a hillside and loosens soil.
changes as the soil becomes easier to move.

columns.title: What changed next?

items.rest: loose soil moves into a river and makes the water muddy
fish habitat changes because the water conditions change

items: A strong science explanation traces the interaction, not just the labels.

rows.head: Rain washes ash into streams
body: Use this language: affects, leads to, changes, because.

prompts.text: Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack.
Explain at least one interaction chain. Do more than list the spheres.

rows.text: The four spheres help organize Earth systems.
Strong explanations trace how one change affects another.
Earth events are not isolated facts. They are system interactions.

goals: I can describe the four main Earth spheres.
I can identify when more than one sphere is involved in an event or change.
I can explain how a change in one sphere can affect another.

anchor: Earth scientists use the words **atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, and biosphere** to organize what is happening in the natural world.
In this lesson, you are not just naming categories. You are looking for how one change can affect another part of the Earth system.
supported.tip: When you get stuck, ask: What changed first? What changed next? Which sphere was affected?

questions.q_text: A hillside gets heavy rain. Soil washes into a river. Name two spheres involved and write one example for each.
Sort these examples into the sphere they fit best: rainfall, fish, soil, smoke, groundwater, tree roots.
Complete this idea: Heavy rain affects the ______ sphere first. Then it affects the ______ sphere because ______.
Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack. Explain one way a change in one sphere could affect another.

game.title: Extra-time chain builder
lines: Use only after Q1–Q4 are finished.
1. Start with one event: heavy rain / wildfire / drought
2. Add one sphere that changes first.
3. Add a second sphere that changes next.

self_check.headline: Did you name the four main spheres accurately?
Can you tell when more than one sphere is involved in one event?
Did you explain at least one connection instead of only listing spheres?
Does your event answer show a real cause-and-effect link?

answer_key.criterion: Student names two relevant spheres and gives a fitting example for each.
notes: Accept rain as atmosphere or hydrosphere depending on explanation.
Look for land/soil, water, air, or living things.

answer_key.criterion: Most examples are sorted accurately enough.
notes: rainfall → hydrosphere
fish/tree roots → biosphere
soil → geosphere
smoke → atmosphere
groundwater → hydrosphere

answer_key.criterion: Student names at least two spheres and explains how one affects another.
notes: Simple because-language is enough here.
The answer should be more than two labels.

answer_key.criterion: Student explains one plausible interaction between spheres in the chosen event.
notes: Accept any reasonable event chain.
Stronger answers show what changed and why it mattered.

anchor: The biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere are interconnected. Scientists use these categories to explain how matter and energy move through Earth systems.
A strong explanation does more than label the spheres involved in an event. It traces how one change affects another and uses scientific reasoning to show the connection.
In this lesson, you will sort examples, explain an interaction chain, and apply the model to a real or realistic event.

questions.q_text: Define each of the four main Earth spheres and give one example for each.
Sort these examples into the sphere they fit best and justify one tricky choice: rainfall, fish, rock, clouds, tree roots, glacier melt, soil, algae, smoke, groundwater.
Explain how heavy rain on a hillside can affect more than one sphere. Use at least two because/affects statements.
Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack. Identify the spheres involved and explain at least one interaction chain clearly.

game.title: Quick systems sorter
lines: Use only after Q1–Q4 are finished.
3. Add a second and third linked change.
4. Circle the strongest scientific verb: affects / increases / reduces / changes.

self_check.headline: Did you define the four spheres clearly and accurately?
hints: do not mix up water and air

self_check.headline: Did you justify at least one sorting choice?
Does your explanation show a real chain of effects?
Does your event analysis explain a connection rather than only listing categories?

answer_key.criterion: Definitions and examples match the four spheres accurately.
notes: Atmosphere = air/gases/weather
Hydrosphere = water in liquid, ice, or vapour form
Geosphere = rocks/soil/land
Biosphere = living things

answer_key.criterion: Sorting is mostly accurate and the justification is reasonable.
notes: glacier melt and groundwater → hydrosphere
smoke/clouds → atmosphere
algae/fish/tree roots → biosphere
rock/soil → geosphere

answer_key.criterion: Student explains how one change moves through at least two spheres.
notes: Look for a real sequence, not separate facts.
Because-language should connect the steps.

answer_key.criterion: Student identifies the spheres involved and explains at least one clear interaction chain.
notes: Stronger answers show cause and consequence clearly.
Accept multiple reasonable chains if the science makes sense.

anchor: The biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere are interconnected, as matter cycles and energy flows through them.

The sphere model is useful because it helps scientists explain systems, not just describe separate parts. Real events often involve overlapping causes, multiple effects, and trade-offs in how we interpret what matters most.
In this lesson, use the model critically: classify examples, explain a chain, and evaluate why the model is stronger than a simple list of observations.

questions.q_text: Explain why the sphere model is more useful than describing a natural event as one isolated fact. Use an example.
Sort the examples and explain one case that could seem to fit more than one sphere: rainfall, fish, rock, clouds, tree roots, glacier melt, soil, algae, smoke, groundwater.
Analyze the wildfire smoke → plant damage → ash in streams example. Explain the interaction chain with precise scientific language.
Using the Big Idea wording, argue why understanding Earth systems as interconnected leads to stronger scientific explanations than simply naming land, air, water, and living things.

self_check.headline: Did you explain why the model matters, not just what the model includes?
Did you handle an ambiguous case with a reasoned explanation?
Is your interaction chain precise and clearly sequenced?
hints: show at least two linked effects

self_check.headline: Did you use the Big Idea wording in a meaningful way?

answer_key.criterion: Student explains that the model helps organize and explain interactions across Earth systems.
notes: A strong answer contrasts isolated description with systems explanation.
An example should show why the model improves understanding.

answer_key.criterion: Sorting is accurate enough and the ambiguous case is justified thoughtfully.
notes: Accept good reasoning when a student notes overlap.
The final fit should still be defensible.

answer_key.criterion: Student explains the wildfire example as a precise chain across multiple spheres.
notes: Atmosphere → biosphere → hydrosphere is a common path.
Stronger answers show cause, not just sequence.

answer_key.criterion: Student uses the Big Idea wording to argue that interconnected systems lead to stronger explanations.
notes: Look for explicit use of interconnected plus matter/energy language.
This is an argument, not a definition question.

rubrics.title: Q4 — Event analysis
rubrics.question: Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack. Identify the spheres involved and explain at least one interaction chain clearly.

criteria.emerging: Names some relevant parts of the event but gives little explanation of how they connect.
criteria.developing: Identifies the spheres involved and gives a partial explanation of one interaction.
criteria.proficient: Clearly explains at least one interaction chain using accurate sphere language and cause-effect reasoning.
criteria.extending: Explains the interaction chain clearly and precisely, showing why the system model strengthens the explanation.

criteria.emerging: Response is vague or mostly a list.
criteria.developing: Response includes some detail but may be uneven or partly list-like.
criteria.proficient: Response is clear, organized, and tied to the chosen event.
criteria.extending: Response is precise, well-structured, and uses strong scientific language throughout.

rubrics.title: Q4 — Big Idea argument
rubrics.question: Using the Big Idea wording, argue why understanding Earth systems as interconnected leads to stronger scientific explanations than simply naming land, air, water, and living things.

criteria.emerging: References the idea loosely or incompletely.
criteria.developing: Uses some of the wording but does not fully connect it to the argument.
criteria.proficient: Uses the Big Idea accurately to support the argument.
criteria.extending: Uses the Big Idea precisely and integrates it smoothly into a strong argument.

criteria.emerging: Reasoning is limited or mostly descriptive.
criteria.developing: Reasoning shows some understanding but lacks full explanation.
criteria.proficient: Reasoning clearly explains why interconnected systems produce stronger scientific explanations.
criteria.extending: Reasoning is nuanced, well-supported, and clearly contrasts systems thinking with simple listing.

target_understanding: Students understand that the four spheres are a model for organizing Earth systems.
Students can identify when more than one sphere is involved in one event.
Students can explain at least one interaction chain instead of only naming categories.
observation_targets: ☐ Students move from labels to cause-and-effect explanations
☐ Students use atmosphere / hydrosphere / geosphere / biosphere accurately in discussion and writing

watch_for.misconception: Students think the spheres are separate boxes with no overlap.
watch_for.redirect: What changed first, and what did that change affect next?

watch_for.misconception: Students list spheres without explaining how they interact.
watch_for.redirect: Can you show the connection with affects, leads to, or because?

forward_connection: Students can use the sphere model later when studying weather, erosion, water systems, climate, or human impacts on ecosystems.

timing.activity: Introduce system idea and the four spheres
Model and discuss one interaction chain
Worksheet event analysis and guided support

differentiation_notes: ● Supported: smaller example set, visible reminder language, and guided chain sentence stem.
◆ Proficient: standard event-analysis response with clearer independence.
▲ Extending: stronger justification, ambiguous-case handling, and Big Idea argument.
teacher_notes: Keep the opening concrete. Do not start with a vocabulary dump.
The key move is from naming categories to explaining interactions.
Use local or familiar events where possible to make transfer easier.
